expectations.text: Notes that by late teens, most lifters should be capable of solid strength gains (age is not the main limiter)
expectations.evidence: 'Being 16-19 does not hurt strength progress. In fact, teenagers typically have advantages for building strength.' Lists hormonal advantages, neural adaptability, and recovery capacity.

expectations.text: Emphasizes huge individual variability in where linear progression stalls
expectations.evidence: 'Individual variation is huge: Starting strength, body weight, limb length, and training history all matter. Two 17-year-olds can have very different strength ceilings.'

expectations.text: States a specific benchmark (like 405) is not a reliable readiness cutoff
expectations.evidence: 'Missing a 405 lb deadlift on linear progression is not unusual' and '405 lbs is a legitimate strength milestone that takes time to reach.' Frames it as normal, not a readiness marker.

expectations.text: Recommends using progression timescale (weekly, monthly, etc.) rather than absolute load to classify training stage
expectations.evidence: Mentions 'Linear progression stops working after 3-6 months' and suggests switching to periodized programming, but does not introduce the concept of using progression timescale (session-to-session vs weekly vs monthly) to classify training stage as Greg specifically recommended.
